structured_choices.data_source: Daily patient admission counts from electronic health records across a multi-hospital network, aggregated to monthly level, spanning January 2022 to December 2022 (12 months). Additional features: holiday indicators (binary, derived from calendar), weather data (temperature, precipitation from national weather service), lagged admission trends (1-month, 3-month, 6-month rolling means), and hospital-level metadata (bed capacity, patient demographics, teaching status). Source: production EHR systems with data validation flags for completeness.
structured_choices.label_definition: Monthly total patient admission count per hospital (continuous numeric outcome). For operational evaluation, outcomes are also classified as 'within 10% of actual' (binary: success/failure) to align with the stakeholder's operational threshold.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with hospital-level stratification. Training: January–August 2022 (8 months). Validation: September–October 2022 (2 months). Test: November–December 2022 (2 months). Rationale: Temporal splits preserve the sequential structure of time series data and prevent future information leakage. The 8:2:2 ratio provides sufficient training depth for RNN architectures while reserving recent, held-out data for realistic performance assessment. Hospital-level stratification ensures train/val/test each contain admission time series from all hospitals in balanced proportions, controlling for hospital-size and demographic confounds. This design avoids the risk of training on one set of hospitals and testing on entirely different ones, which would conflate model generalization with inter-hospital variability.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, lagged feature construction) are fit on the training set only (Jan–Aug 2022). Fitted parameters (mean, std, forward-fill masks) are then applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage: the model never 'sees' validation or test statistics during training. Lagged features are constructed using only historical data up to each month (no future information used).
structured_choices.features: 1. Admission counts (lagged: t-1, t-3, t-6 months). 2. Rolling statistics: 3-month and 6-month rolling means and standard deviations of admissions (computed using only prior months). 3. Holiday indicator (binary, derived from calendar). 4. Weather features (temperature, precipitation; standardized using training set statistics). 5. Hospital-level features: bed capacity (standardized), teaching hospital status (binary), and demographic composition (proportion of pediatric, elderly, chronic disease patients; standardized). 6. Seasonal decomposition: month-of-year one-hot encoding (categorical features for Jan–Dec). No feature encodes the future label or contains information unavailable at prediction time.
structured_choices.model: RNN architecture: LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) with 2 stacked layers (128 and 64 units), dropout (0.2), batch normalization, and a dense output layer. Input: sequences of 6 months of lagged features; output: single-step prediction for the next month's admissions. Hyperparameters tuned via grid search on validation set: learning rate (0.001, 0.0005), batch size (16, 32), optimizer (Adam, RMSprop). Training uses early stopping (patience=10 epochs) based on validation MAE.
structured_choices.baseline: ARIMA(p,d,q) model fitted independently to each hospital's admission time series. Baseline hyperparameters (p, d, q) selected via auto.arima using AIC on the training set. The baseline receives identical feature engineering (no exogenous variables for ARIMA baseline to maintain simplicity and fair comparison; ARIMA relies on admission history alone). ARIMA is tuned with the same computational budget as the RNN: grid search over plausible (p, d, q) ranges using training data. Both models are evaluated on identical validation and test sets under identical metrics. Rationale: ARIMA is the traditional state-of-the-art time series forecasting method; comparing RNN to ARIMA isolates the benefit of neural sequence modeling versus classical statistical forecasting.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) on the test set, aggregated across all hospitals. MAPE is chosen because: (1) it is scale-invariant, making it comparable across hospitals of different sizes; (2) it aligns with the operational objective of 'predictions within 10% of actual' — low MAPE directly reflects the ability to hit this threshold; (3) percentage error is interpretable to hospital operations teams. MAPE is computed as mean(|actual − predicted| / |actual|) × 100 across all test months and hospitals.
secondary_metrics: Percentage of predictions within 10% of actual (binary success rate) — direct operational metric.
Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) on test set — penalizes larger errors, useful for detecting outliers.
Mean Absolute Error (MAE) per hospital — to check whether model performance is uniform or driven by a subset of hospitals.
Directional accuracy — binary: did the model correctly predict whether admissions increased or decreased month-over-month?
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (Sep–Oct 2022). Hyperparameters for both RNN and ARIMA are selected by evaluating MAPE on the validation set. The model (RNN or ARIMA) exhibiting the lowest validation MAPE is carried forward to the test set. The test set is held-out and used only once: to compute the final reported metrics. No tuning or model selection occurs on the test set.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (Nov–Dec 2022) is sealed during model development and hyperparameter tuning. After both models (RNN and ARIMA) are finalized using validation data, the test set is used exactly once to compute primary and secondary metrics. No model re-training, re-tuning, or selection occurs on test data. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap resampling across hospitals).
structured_choices.confound_controls: 1. External event log: A domain expert reviews the Nov–Dec 2022 period (test window) and the Jan–Aug 2022 period (training window) for documented external events (disease outbreaks, policy changes, natural disasters) that might affect admissions. Any such events are flagged and reported in the results section; if present in the test set, they are noted as caveats to generalization. 2. Stratified evaluation: Metrics are reported separately by hospital size (tertiles of bed capacity) to detect if the RNN advantage is driven by a specific hospital class or confounded by hospital size. 3. Pre/post-event analysis: If external events are identified, analyses are repeated excluding those months as a sensitivity check. 4. Seasonal stability check: 2022 is checked against 2021 admission patterns (if available) to verify that seasonal trends are typical, not anomalous. 5. Hospital-level residual analysis: Residuals from both models are examined by hospital to check for systematic bias in any one hospital, which could indicate unmeasured local confounds.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RNN vs. ARIMA for Hospital Admission Forecasting

### 1. Data and Outcome Definition

The experiment uses monthly patient admission counts from a multi-hospital network spanning January to December 2022, sourced from production electronic health records. The outcome is the total monthly admission count per hospital (continuous). For operational alignment, we will additionally report the percentage of predictions that fall within ±10% of actual admissions.

Features include: lagged admission counts (1-, 3-, and 6-month lags), rolling 3- and 6-month statistics, holiday indicators, weather data (temperature, precipitation), and hospital-level metadata (bed capacity, teaching status, demographic composition). All features are constructed to use only historical information available at prediction time; no future information leaks into feature engineering.

### 2. Split Strategy and Temporal Design

Data is split temporally to preserve time-series structure and prevent leakage: **training (Jan–Aug 2022), validation (Sep–Oct 2022), test (Nov–Dec 2022)**. This 8:2:2 allocation provides sufficient training depth for RNN convergence while reserving recent, unseen data for realistic performance assessment.

Critically, the split is **stratified by hospital** — each set contains admission time series from all hospitals in balanced proportions. This ensures that model generalization is tested on the same hospital population used for training, isolating the forecasting algorithm's merit from inter-hospital variability confounds.

No data leakage occurs: preprocessing statistics (scaling parameters, forward-fill masks) are fit on training data only and applied to validation and test sets identically.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All transformers are fit on the 8-month training window and locked before evaluation. Standardization uses training set moments. Lagged features are computed using only prior months' data; no future information is introduced. Seasonal effects are captured via month-of-year one-hot encoding. Hospital-level features (bed capacity, demographics) are standardized using training set statistics.

### 4. Model and Baseline Specifications

**RNN Model:** LSTM with 2 stacked layers (128 and 64 units), dropout regularization (0.2), batch normalization, and a single-step output layer. Input is a sequence of 6 months of lagged features. Hyperparameters (learning rate ∈ {0.001, 0.0005}, batch size ∈ {16, 32}, optimizer ∈ {Adam, RMSprop}) are tuned via grid search on validation data, with early stopping (patience=10) based on validation MAE.

**Baseline Model:** ARIMA(p,d,q) fitted independently to each hospital's admission time series using auto.arima (AIC criterion) on the training set alone. The baseline receives no exogenous variables (only admission history), keeping it methodologically standard. ARIMA hyperparameters are selected with equivalent computational effort as the RNN: grid search over plausible (p, d, q) ranges on training data.

Both models are evaluated under identical conditions on identical data, ensuring fair comparison.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) on the test set, aggregated across all hospitals and months. MAPE is scale-invariant (comparable across hospitals of different sizes) and directly aligns with the operational goal of ±10% prediction accuracy. It is computed as mean(|actual − predicted| / |actual|) × 100.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Percentage of predictions within ±10% of actual (binary operational success rate)
- RMSE (penalizes larger errors; sensitive to outliers)
- MAE per hospital (detects if performance is uniform or hospital-dependent)
- Directional accuracy (month-over-month trend prediction)

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Model selection occurs entirely on the validation set (Sep–Oct 2022). The model with lowest validation MAPE is selected. The test set is sealed until final evaluation and is used exactly once to report results. No re-tuning occurs on test data.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

External events (disease outbreaks, policy changes) are identified via domain expert review and logged. If present in the test window, they are reported as caveats. Metrics are stratified by hospital size (bed capacity tertiles) to detect confounding by hospital class. Sensitivity analyses exclude flagged external event months. A pre-existing seasonal stability check (2021 vs. 2022 patterns, if available) verifies that 2022 trends are typical. Residual analysis by hospital detects systematic bias or unmeasured local confounds.

**Scope of claim:** This design will establish whether RNNs outperform ARIMA for multi-hospital admission forecasting under 2022 conditions. It does not establish causality, generalization to future years without re-training, or robustness to regime-changing external events occurring after model finalization.
